name: Foster & Hume
short_description: Classic Missouri rockhounding site offering easy access to quartz, chert, and jasper specimens in flat, open terrain near New Home.
description:
Foster & Hume is a productive rockhounding location in southwestern Missouri's Bates County, situated in the Osage Plains physiographic region. This flat, accessible site has long been known to local rockhounders for yielding quality specimens of milky quartz, translucent chert, and colorful jasper fragments. The area reflects Missouri's rich geological heritage from the Paleozoic era, when shallow seas and periodic flood events deposited thick sequences of sedimentary rocks rich in silica.

The site's primary appeal is its ease of access and abundance of surface material. Rockhounders can find specimens scattered across open ground and in eroded wash areas, making this an ideal location for beginners and families. The flat terrain and relatively short walking distances from parking make it manageable for collectors of varying fitness levels.

Collecting here focuses on surface finds and shallow prospecting in disturbed earth. The chert specimens often show the characteristic translucency and waxy luster typical of Missouri's Cherokee Group formations. Jasper ranges from solid colors to banded varieties, while quartz pieces may include both clear and milky specimens with occasional smoky or rose-tinted examples.

This location is best visited during cooler months when the open terrain offers natural shade relief is minimal. The relatively remote location rewards patience and systematic searching, with most productive areas occurring near old erosional features and along slight drainage patterns across the flatter ground.
history: Foster & Hume sits within Missouri's Paleozoic sedimentary formations, specifically the Osage Group and Cherokee Group strata dating to the Carboniferous period. Silica-rich solutions percolating through these formations during diagenesis created abundant chert nodules and jasper seams, while quartz crystallized in fractures and cavities. Local rockhounding interest in this area developed through the 20th century as collectors discovered the consistent quality and quantity of specimens available at the surface.
tips: Search the eroded drainage swales and low spots where erosion naturally concentrates heavier mineral specimens. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and hand tools (small chisel, digging bar) to test promising areas just below the surface where fresh material is more abundant. Look for chert with a waxy, translucent appearance and search for jasper near contact zones where soil color changes, as these boundaries often indicate fresh material exposure.
terrain_notes: Elevation 877 feet with very flat to gently sloping terrain throughout the site. Open prairie-like landscape with sparse vegetation and low brush. Walking distances from parking are typically short (under 0.5 miles), and footing is generally stable across firm soil and sparse grass. No significant obstacles, though watch for occasional loose rocks scattered across the surface.
best_season: October - April. Missouri's hot, humid summers (June - August) make this open terrain uncomfortable, and seasonal vegetation growth can obscure surface finds. Fall through early spring offers ideal conditions with cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and vegetation dormancy that makes specimens more visible.
written_directions: From New Home, head south on local roads toward the Bates County township roads. The site is situated approximately 3-4 miles south of New Home near the Foster & Hume locality. Use GPS coordinates 38.156535, -94.494156 as your navigation target. Approach via county roads suitable for passenger vehicles; the final approach may be on unpaved county or township roads. Confirm local access before visiting, as some adjacent properties are private.
road_conditions: County and township roads in good condition; final approach unpaved but passable for standard vehicles in normal conditions. No high-clearance vehicles required.
parking_notes: Informal pullout areas near the site. No developed parking lot; park off the road in designated informal areas. Space available for 3-5 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use collection only. Respect private property boundaries; this location sits on public/open land but is surrounded by private property in some directions. Remove only loose surface material and small specimens. Do not excavate, blast, or use motorized equipment. Leave the site as you found it. Always verify current access status before visiting.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collecting
No motorized excavation
Respect surrounding private property boundaries
Remove surface material only
Leave site as you found it
Verify access before visiting
nearest_services: New Home (3-4 miles north) - limited services. Osceola (approximately 12 miles) offers gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Very accessible site with flat terrain and short walking distances from parking (typically under 0.5 miles). Firm soil footing suitable for most mobility levels. No significant obstacles. Suitable for families with young children and older collectors.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Foster & Hume in Missouri?
faq.answer: Foster & Hume is known for yielding quartz, chert, jasper, and calcite specimens. Chert is particularly abundant and often shows translucent, waxy characteristics typical of Missouri's Osage Group formations. Jasper occurs in solid colors and banded varieties, while quartz ranges from clear to milky specimens.

faq.question: Is Foster & Hume a good rockhounding location for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Foster & Hume is excellent for beginners. The site features flat, easy terrain with minimal walking distance from parking, abundant surface material scattered across open ground, and no permit requirements. Families and inexperienced rockhounders can find quality specimens with basic hand tools.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Foster & Hume near New Home?
faq.answer: October through April offers ideal conditions. Missouri's summers are hot and humid, making the open terrain uncomfortable. Fall through early spring provides cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and dormant vegetation that makes surface specimens more visible and easier to locate.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks at Foster & Hume, Missouri?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collection at Foster & Hume. However, confirm current access status before visiting, as the site is surrounded by private property in some directions. Respect all property boundaries and collect only surface material from accessible public areas.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring rockhounding at Foster & Hume?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket for specimens, a small digging bar or chisel for testing promising spots just below the surface, and a screen or classifier to sort material. Wear sturdy footwear and bring water, sunscreen, and a hat for protection during cooler months when you may spend extended time in open terrain.

faq.question: How do I find chert and jasper at Foster & Hume rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Search eroded drainage swales and low spots where erosion naturally concentrates specimens. Look for chert with waxy, translucent appearance and search for jasper near soil color change boundaries, as these contact zones indicate fresh material exposure. Test shallow areas just below the surface with a digging bar to find material not yet weathered.
